21.correct: The Seventh Schedule of the Constitution divides legislative powers between the Union and the States through three lists - the Union List, the State List and the Concurrent List.

wrong.A: The Fifth Schedule deals with the administration of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes.
wrong.C: The Ninth Schedule contains laws protected from judicial review (mainly land-reform laws).
wrong.D: The Tenth Schedule contains the anti-defection provisions.

21.fact: The Union List has the most subjects, and on the Concurrent List a Union law prevails over a conflicting State law.

22.correct: The Governor is administered the oath of office by the Chief Justice of the High Court of that state (or, in his absence, the senior-most judge available).

wrong.A: The President appoints the Governor but does not administer the oath.
wrong.B: The Prime Minister has no role in swearing in a Governor.
wrong.D: The Chief Minister is himself sworn in by the Governor, not the other way round.

22.fact: The President, by contrast, is sworn in by the Chief Justice of India.

23.correct: The defining feature of the Tippani dance (Gujarat) is the use of long sticks called 'tippani', which the women dancers strike rhythmically on the ground while dancing.

wrong.B: Sword-and-shield fencing describes martial dances like Paika, not Tippani.
wrong.C: Tippani is a lively group dance, not a slow solo performance.
wrong.D: Circular use of a handkerchief is not a feature of the Tippani dance.

23.fact: The dance grew out of the labour of women who pounded floors firm with the tippani stick.

24.correct: Kalbeliya is the traditional folk dance of Rajasthan, performed by the Kalbeliya (snake-charmer) community; it is on UNESCO's Intangible Cultural Heritage list.

wrong.A: Gujarat is known for Garba and Dandiya, not Kalbeliya.
wrong.C: Punjab's signature folk dances are Bhangra and Gidda.
wrong.D: Maharashtra is associated with Lavani and Tamasha.

24.fact: Kalbeliya dancers wear flowing black skirts and move in serpentine patterns imitating a snake.

25.correct: Nathu La is a high mountain pass in Sikkim that connects India with Tibet (China) and lies on an old branch of the Silk Route.

wrong.B: Rohtang Pass is in Himachal Pradesh, linking the Kullu valley with Lahaul-Spiti.
wrong.C: Shipki La is a pass in Himachal Pradesh, not Sikkim.
wrong.D: Zoji La connects Srinagar with Leh in the Ladakh region.

25.fact: Nathu La was reopened for border trade in 2006 after being closed since the 1962 war.

26.correct: Mawsynram in Meghalaya receives the highest average rainfall in India (and the world), as the funnel-shaped Khasi hills force monsoon winds to rise and drop heavy rain.

wrong.A: The Kerala coast gets heavy monsoon rain but far less than Mawsynram.
wrong.C: The Konkan coast is wet but does not match Mawsynram's totals.
wrong.D: Western Rajasthan (Thar desert) is one of the driest parts of India.

26.fact: Nearby Cherrapunji (Sohra) also records extremely high rainfall, both fed by the same Khasi-hill orographic effect.

27.correct: 'Ghare-Baire' (The Home and the World), which critically examines the Swadeshi movement, was written by Rabindranath Tagore.

wrong.A: Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay wrote 'Anandamath' and 'Vande Mataram', from an earlier generation.
wrong.B: Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay wrote novels like 'Devdas' and 'Parineeta'.
wrong.D: Bhabani Bhattacharya was a later English-language Indian novelist.

27.fact: Tagore won the Nobel Prize for Literature in 1913, the first non-European to do so.

28.correct: Suniti Choudhury, a teenage revolutionary, was about 14 when she (with Shanti Ghosh) shot dead the British magistrate of Comilla in 1931.

wrong.B: Tara Rani Srivastava was a Quit-India-movement activist, not the magistrate's assassin.
wrong.C: Matangini Hazara was a Gandhian who died in the 1942 movement.
wrong.D: Aruna Asaf Ali is famous for hoisting the flag during the 1942 Quit India Movement.

28.fact: Suniti Choudhury was among the youngest revolutionaries imprisoned in the Indian freedom struggle.

29.correct: Puducherry (Pondicherry) is famous for its French Quarter, where colonial French and Indian (Tamil) architectural styles blend in its streets and buildings.

wrong.A: Mumbai is known for Gothic, Art-Deco and Indo-Saracenic architecture, not a French quarter.
wrong.D: Chennai (Madras) has British and Indo-Saracenic buildings, not a French quarter.

29.fact: Puducherry was a French colony until 1954 and still has French-named streets and a French institute.

30.correct: Uranus has an axial tilt of about 98 degrees, so it rotates almost on its side; this produces extreme seasonal variation, with each pole facing the Sun continuously for about 42 years.

wrong.B: With such an extreme tilt there are very strong seasonal changes, not none.
wrong.C: The seasons are extreme, not mild.
wrong.D: While a pole does get long days, the overall effect is extreme seasonal change, which option A captures best.

30.fact: Uranus's extreme tilt is thought to have resulted from a massive collision early in the solar system's history.

31.correct: Light from a star 100 light-years away takes 100 years to reach Earth, so if the star explodes now, we would see the explosion only after 100 years.

wrong.A: Light is not instantaneous; it travels at a finite speed.
wrong.C: One year is far too short for light to cross 100 light-years.
wrong.D: We will eventually see it - after the 100-year light-travel time.

31.fact: Because of this delay, when we look at distant stars we are effectively seeing them as they were in the past.

32.correct: Boxer Mary Kom won a bronze medal at the 2012 London Olympics, becoming the first Indian woman boxer to win an Olympic medal.

wrong.A: Women's boxing was not part of the 2008 Beijing Olympics.
wrong.C: Mary Kom did not win a medal at Rio 2016.
wrong.D: She did not medal at Tokyo 2020.

32.fact: Mary Kom is a six-time World Amateur Boxing champion, an achievement unmatched by any other boxer.

33.correct: India won 7 medals at the Tokyo 2020 Olympics (1 gold, 2 silver, 4 bronze) - its best-ever Olympic medal haul.

wrong.B: 6 is one short of the actual count.
wrong.D: 8 is one more than India actually won.

33.fact: Neeraj Chopra's javelin gold at Tokyo 2020 was India's first-ever Olympic gold in athletics.

34.correct: Bone is a type of connective tissue - it connects and supports other tissues and has a hard matrix of collagen and calcium salts.

wrong.A: Muscular tissue is made of contractile fibres for movement.
wrong.D: Epithelial tissue forms protective linings and coverings.

34.fact: Other connective tissues include blood, cartilage and adipose (fat) tissue.

35.correct: The primary function of the chloroplast is photosynthesis - it contains chlorophyll, which captures sunlight to make food (glucose) from carbon dioxide and water.

wrong.A: Starch storage is a secondary role; the chloroplast's main job is photosynthesis.
wrong.B: Protein synthesis is carried out by ribosomes.
wrong.C: Energy production by respiration is the work of mitochondria, not chloroplasts.

35.fact: Chloroplasts are found only in plant cells and some algae, giving plants their green colour.

36.correct: The Western Ghats are a UNESCO World Heritage Site because of their exceptionally rich biodiversity and high number of endemic (found-nowhere-else) species.

wrong.A: They are recognised for biodiversity, not for mineral resources.
wrong.C: Their listing is natural, not for ancient monuments.
wrong.D: It is a natural heritage site, not listed for cultural significance.

36.fact: The Western Ghats are one of the world's eight 'hottest hotspots' of biological diversity.

37.correct: In June 2024, the Union Cabinet approved construction of an additional 2 crore houses under PMAY-Gramin for the period 2024-25 to 2028-29.

wrong.B: 3 crore is not the additional figure approved for PMAY-G in this decision.
wrong.D: 5 crore is far higher than the sanctioned additional target.

37.fact: PMAY-G aims to provide pucca houses with basic amenities to all eligible rural households.

38.correct: Begum Hazrat Mahal led and inspired the 1857 revolt against the British in Lucknow (Awadh), fighting on behalf of her young son Birjis Qadr.

wrong.A: Nana Saheb led the revolt at Kanpur, not Lucknow.
wrong.C: Rani Lakshmibai led the uprising at Jhansi.
wrong.D: Bahadur Shah Zafar was the symbolic leader at Delhi, not Lucknow.

38.fact: After the revolt failed, Begum Hazrat Mahal took refuge in Nepal, where she died in 1879.

39.correct: The British recaptured Delhi from the rebels in September 1857, after a prolonged siege, ending the symbolic centre of the revolt.

wrong.A: In June 1857 the revolt was still spreading; Delhi was in rebel hands.
wrong.C: By March 1858 the focus had shifted to Lucknow's recapture.
wrong.D: July 1858 is after Delhi had already fallen.

39.fact: After Delhi's fall, Bahadur Shah Zafar was captured and later exiled to Rangoon.

40.correct: According to the SIPRI report on arms transfers (2020-24), Asia and Oceania accounted for about 33% of all global arms imports, the largest regional share.

wrong.B: 50% overstates the region's share of arms imports.

40.fact: SIPRI (Stockholm International Peace Research Institute) tracks global military spending and arms transfers.
